title: Maamoul mad
subtitle: Levantine semolina tray cake with a date filling, cut into squares.
excerpt: Maamoul mad is the tray-baked version of maamoul, the Levantine festival cookie. Instead of shaping individual cookies in a mould, the semolina dough is pressed into a rectangular tin, the date filling is spread over it, and a second dough layer goes on top. It bakes as a tray and is cut into squares or diamonds. The texture is tender and crumbly from the semolina, and the date layer is sweet and fudge-like.
sourceNotes: Maamoul are traditional celebration cookies across Lebanon, Syria, Palestine, Jordan, and Egypt, eaten at Eid and Easter. The individual moulded versions require wooden or plastic presses to create the traditional patterns; maamoul mad (meaning 'crazy' or 'spread out' maamoul) uses the same dough and filling but bakes it as a flat tray, eliminating the shaping step. This format is particularly popular in Lebanon. The semolina dough is flavoured with rose water and orange blossom water. The filling is typically date paste, though nut fillings are also traditional.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in an airtight tin layered between baking paper. Defrost at room temperature for an hour.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in a tin at room temperature for up to a week. Improves on the second and third day as the semolina softens.

recipeTools.notes: 25 x 35 cm tin, greased and lined.

content.text: The dough is made with semolina rather than flour, which gives it its characteristic crumbly, slightly gritty texture. It needs an hour to rest after mixing so the semolina can absorb the liquid fully; if you skip the rest, the dough crumbles when you press it into the tin.
The date filling is a simple paste of dates, butter, and spice, blended until smooth. If the dates are very dry, warm them in a little water for 5 minutes first to soften them.

Mix {{semolina}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{baking-powder}} together in a large bowl. Pour in the melted {{unsalted-butter}} (dough quantity) and mix with your hands until the semolina is evenly coated. It will look sandy. Add {{rose-water}} and {{orange-blossom-water}} and mix again. Cover and leave to rest for 1 hour.

Blend {{dates-medjool}} with {{unsalted-butter}} (filling quantity) and {{cinnamon-ground}} in a food processor until smooth. If the mixture is stiff, add a tablespoon of warm water. The paste should be soft and spreadable.
Heat the oven to 180°C fan (200°C conventional). Grease and line the baking tin.

Divide the rested dough in half. Press the first half into the base of the tin in an even layer, using wet fingers or a flat-bottomed glass. Spread the date paste over the top in an even layer, right to the edges.

Press the remaining dough into a flat slab on a piece of baking paper (the same size as the tin), then invert it over the date layer. Press gently to stick. Scatter {{almonds-flaked}} over the surface and press in lightly.
Bake for 28 to 32 minutes until golden on top. Leave to cool completely in the tin before cutting into squares or diamonds. The texture firms up as it cools; do not cut while warm.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Texture is the main thing to watch.

items.symptom: Dough too crumbly to press into the tin.
items.cause: Resting time skipped, or not enough fat.
items.fix: Rest for the full hour. If still crumbly, add a tablespoon of warm water and mix again.
Wait until completely cool. The semolina sets as it cools. Cut with a sharp knife in one clean downward motion.
